sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable games from the year, led by month-specific anchors when the evidence supports them.

items.title: Wolfenstein 3D
items.body: id Software makes the PC corridor fast, violent and shareable.
items.sourceNote: May 5, 1992 release date is widely cited.

items.title: Street Fighter II: Champion Edition
items.body: Capcom updates the cabinet everyone is already crowding around.
items.sourceNote: March 1992 arcade release is commonly cited.

items.title: Kirby's Dream Land
items.body: HAL's soft, readable handheld platformer introduces Kirby.
items.sourceNote: April 1992 Japanese release is commonly cited.

items.title: Super Mario Kart
items.body: Nintendo turns Mode 7 racing into a living-room multiplayer ritual.
items.sourceNote: Japanese release date is widely cited.

items.title: Mortal Kombat
items.body: Midway's digitised fighters add gore, secrets and controversy to the arcade.
items.sourceNote: October 1992 arcade timing is commonly cited.

items.title: Super Mario Land 2: 6 Golden Coins
items.body: Mario's handheld world grows stranger and introduces Wario.
items.sourceNote: October 1992 Japanese date is commonly cited.

items.title: Sonic the Hedgehog 2
items.body: Sega turns a sequel into a global retail event.
items.sourceNote: November 1992 release context is secure; exact regional date varies.

items.title: Dune II
items.body: Westwood codifies much of the real-time strategy grammar.
items.sourceNote: December 1992 DOS release is commonly cited.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Machines, formats and buying context around the exhibit month.

items.title: SNES arrives in Europe
items.body: Nintendo's 16-bit machine finally becomes a European retail reality, with price and cartridge costs part of the conversation.
items.sourceNote: 1992 European launch is secure; exact local dates varied by market.

items.title: Mega Drive has momentum in Britain
items.body: Sonic 2 and aggressive Sega marketing make the Mega Drive feel like the sharper teenage choice.
items.sourceNote: Sonic 2 and UK retail context.

items.title: PC sound cards and hard drives matter more
items.body: Wolfenstein 3D makes the family PC feel more like a game machine if it has the right parts.
items.sourceNote: PC hardware context.

items.title: CD-ROM remains aspirational
items.body: Mega-CD previews and PC multimedia talk suggest a future that is not yet ordinary in most bedrooms.
items.sourceNote: Early 1990s CD-ROM context.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How players found information before search, streams and social feeds.

items.title: Shareware spreads by disk and dial-up
items.body: Wolfenstein 3D demonstrates how a PC game can circulate before a boxed copy is bought.

items.title: Magazine coverdisks gain importance
items.body: For UK players, a disk taped to a magazine can be the cheapest route to the future.

items.title: Cheat culture gets louder
items.body: Mortal Kombat moves secrets, fatalities and rumours into playground performance.

items.title: Forums are not yet everyday
items.body: Most players still meet the wider game world through print, shops and local friends, not a browser.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading of the month from the player side of history.

items.title: Arcades felt dangerous again
items.body: Mortal Kombat made cabinets feel like forbidden theatre.

items.title: PC corridors changed perspective
items.body: Wolfenstein 3D made a monitor feel like it had depth and threat.

items.title: Sonic became appointment retail
items.body: Sonic 2 turned a sequel into an event rather than just another cartridge.

items.title: The SNES felt expensive and beautiful
items.body: Mode 7, Mario and Nintendo polish carried a premium aura on UK shelves.
